cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The user is asking what the history experts' statement proves about the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to go back to the passage and find where the history experts are mentioned. The passage says, "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the general public began singing the 'Happy Birthday' song, but they believe it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company." So the key points here are that the experts can't find who wrote the lyrics and that the song was being sung by the public long before a company owned it. The company mentioned is Warner Music Group, which claims ownership since 1988. The question is about what the experts' statements prove. The options are: A) does not belong to Warner Music Group B) has more than 200 years' history C) was initially owned by another company D) has always been very popular Looking at the passage, the experts don't mention the song being over 200 years old. The original song "Good Morning to All" was written in 1893, so the birthday version came after that. 2023 minus 1893 is 130 years, so B is incorrect. Option C says it was initially owned by another company. The passage doesn't mention that. The Hill sisters wrote the original song, but ownership by a company isn't discussed in that context. So C is out. D says it has always been very popular. The passage mentions that it was being sung by the public long before being owned by a company, but "always been very popular" isn't directly stated. The experts don't talk about popularity, just about when it was adopted by the public. So D might not be the best choice. Option A: The experts say the song was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company.
